departmentReports.summary: landing at Arcadia Planitia.  Radiation exposure is effectively identical at both sites, so the decision turns on health-protective logistics and operational safety.

citations.text: Mars surface radiation averages 0.67 mSv/day, approximately 20x Earth background
citations.context: Radiation is a shared long-term exposure risk at both candidate sites.

citations.text: Arcadia Planitia contains extensive subsurface ice deposits detected by MARSIS radar
citations.context: Supports water access, life support resilience, and lower transport burden.

citations.text: Valles Marineris spans 4,000 km with up to 7 km depth, exposing 3.5 billion years of strata
citations.context: High scientific value, but not directly a health advantage.

citations.text: CRISM spectrometer detected diverse hydrated minerals in Valles Marineris walls
citations.context: Adds exploration value and potential resource interest, but with higher terrain hazards.

citations.text: Valles Marineris terrain slopes up to 30 degrees increase landing risk and construction difficulty
citations.context: Primary reason to avoid as first permanent landing site.

risks.description: Choosing Valles Marineris as the first permanent landing site increases casualty risk from slope instability, rockfall, and difficult surface logistics.
At either site, cumulative radiation remains a major chronic health risk over years and will require shielding, monitoring, and exposure discipline.
With 18 months of food reserves, the colony is not yet highly buffered against delays; a hazardous site would amplify mission fragility.

opportunities.description: Arcadia Planitia’s subsurface ice substantially improves resilience for water, oxygen, agriculture, and contingency planning.
A safe, flat initial site can accelerate module deployment, reduce injury rates, and preserve morale during the critical first year.
Valles Marineris can remain a later science outpost or robotic exploration target once the colony has stronger mobility and medical capacity.

recommendedActions: Land the first permanent settlement at Arcadia Planitia.
Prioritize excavation and shielding around habitats to reduce cumulative radiation exposure.
Use the subsurface ice access to establish robust water recycling, reserve storage, and emergency redundancy.
Delay crewed expansion into high-relief regions until heavy construction assets, rescue capability, and medical throughput improve.
Maintain a robotic science program aimed at Valles Marineris for later mineral and stratigraphic exploration.

siteSelection.reason: Lowest operational health risk with strong water-security advantages.

medicalPlan.radiationMitigation: Habitat regolith shielding, exposure tracking, and EVA scheduling limits.
medicalPlan.injuryMitigation: Flat terrain reduces trauma risk and evacuation complexity.

forgedToolsUsed.name: mars_landing_site_risk_model
forgedToolsUsed.description: Compares Arcadia Planitia and Valles Marineris using colony size, food reserve, power capacity, radiation, and terrain hazard to estimate habitability and mission risk.

openQuestions: What exact landing ellipsoid and local regolith bearing strength measurements are available for Arcadia Planitia?
How quickly can subsurface ice be accessed and processed at the chosen Arcadia site?
What heavy-lift and surface mobility assets will be available after touchdown?

recommendedEffects.description: Select Arcadia Planitia to minimize terrain-related injury, landing, and construction hazards.
Shift early infrastructure planning toward ice extraction, water storage, and shielding at Arcadia.
Defer Valles Marineris as a later science objective after the colony’s medical and engineering capacity matures.

commanderDecision.decision: Establish the first permanent settlement at Arcadia Planitia and build the opening phase around survivability, water security, and rapid shielding. We take the safe plain now so we can earn the right to take bigger swings later.
commanderDecision.rationale: This is a classic expansion trap: the flashy site offers long-term upside, but choosing it at landfall burns margin when our colony is still thin-skinned. Population is only 100, food buffer is 18 months, and medical explicitly flags elevated casualty risk from slope instability, rockfall, and difficult logistics at Valles Marineris. Arcadia gives us flat terrain, cleaner construction tempo, and direct ice access. That means fewer preventable injuries, faster habitat deployment, stronger water redundancy, and earlier radiation shielding. I favor bold moves, but bold is not the same as reckless. The highest-upside play is to found on stable ground, scale infrastructure fast, and use robotics to probe Valles until our rescue, construction, and medical capacity can support a hazardous expansion. We secure the backbone first, then strike outward.
selectedPolicies: Primary settlement at Arcadia Planitia
Immediate focus on ice extraction, water storage, and closed-loop recycling redundancy
Accelerated habitat excavation and shielding program
Robotic exploration of Valles Marineris while delaying permanent crewed expansion there
rejectedPolicies: Immediate permanent settlement at Valles Marineris rim
Early crewed expansion into hazardous high-relief terrain before heavy rescue and construction capability exists
A mineral-first landing posture that sacrifices operational safety margin
expectedTradeoffs: We give up early direct access to Valles mineral potential in exchange for lower casualty risk and faster stable buildout
Morale should rise from choosing the safer, more sustainable site, though a small faction may see deferred canyon expansion as overly cautious
Early engineering effort shifts toward life-support depth and shielding rather than aggressive frontier prospecting
Scientific progress at Valles slows in the short term, but robotic programs preserve momentum without exposing crew
watchMetricsNextTurn: Habitat shielding progress
Water extraction rate from subsurface ice
Recycling efficiency and reserve storage levels
Construction tempo and incident rate
Radiation dose tracking by crew
Food reserve burn rate
Robotic survey returns from Valles Marineris

policyEffectsApplied: Primary settlement at Arcadia Planitia
Immediate focus on ice extraction, water storage, and closed-loop recycling redundancy
Accelerated habitat excavation and shielding program
Robotic exploration of Valles Marineris while delaying permanent crewed expansion there

departmentReports.summary: build the atmospheric water extraction system (WAVAR-type).  The colony is medically and operationally fragile: morale is only 10%, radiation burden is already high, and the water deficit is 20%.

citations.text: Mars subsurface ice confirmed at multiple latitudes by MARSIS and SHARAD radar
citations.context: Supports that subsurface water is real, but not necessarily easy to access at predicted depth.

citations.text: MOXIE on Perseverance demonstrated in-situ oxygen extraction from Mars atmosphere
citations.context: Shows that atmospheric in-situ resource processing is technologically credible on Mars.

citations.text: Mars atmosphere contains 0.03% water vapor, seasonally variable
citations.context: Atmospheric water is sparse but available as a supplemental source.

citations.text: Planetary protection protocols
citations.context: Deep drilling risks contaminating pristine subsurface water reserves.

risks.description: Water deficit is already affecting a 100-person colony with very low morale; failure to stabilize supply could trigger health decline, conflict, and reduced adherence to safety protocols.
Experimental deep drilling could contaminate subsurface aquifers, creating a long-term biosafety and planetary-protection problem.
A 6-month deployment delay means interim water rationing must be strictly managed to avoid dehydration and secondary health effects.

opportunities.description: A WAVAR-type system provides a lower-risk, reliable 20% water deficit closure without threatening pristine reserves.
Reducing technical uncertainty now will improve crew morale and decision confidence in a psychologically stressed colony.
Proven atmospheric recovery infrastructure can be duplicated or expanded later, improving resilience.

recommendedActions: Proceed with the atmospheric water extraction system immediately.
Institute aggressive water rationing and priority allocations during the 6-month build period.
Avoid deep drilling until contamination controls, sterilization, and planetary-protection oversight are stronger.
Monitor hydration status, renal strain, and fatigue markers in all colonists during the interim.
Use the delay to improve storage, recycling efficiency, and leak detection across the water loop.

waterPolicy.rationing: Temporary deficit management until WAVAR comes online.
waterPolicy.priority: Drinking, medical, and crop-support uses first.

biosafety.drillingMoratorium: Pause experimental deep aquifer penetration pending contamination safeguards.

forgedToolsUsed.name: water_supply_decision_model_v2
forgedToolsUsed.description: Compares deep drilling versus atmospheric water extraction using deficit, morale, radiation, food reserve, timeline, and contamination risk with input validation.

openQuestions: How much buffer water is available in storage to cover the 6-month build period?
Can agricultural demand be temporarily reduced without affecting nutrition and morale too severely?
What sterilization and containment procedures are available if deep drilling is revisited later?

recommendedEffects.description: Implement water rationing and medical monitoring during WAVAR deployment to prevent dehydration-related illness.
Add atmospheric water extraction to diversify the colony’s life-support portfolio.
Defer experimental deep aquifer drilling until stronger planetary-protection controls exist.

departmentReports.rationale: The colony is already at 80% of water need, and the shortfall directly constrains agriculture and therefore food security. A 6-month atmospheric extraction system is slower, but it is the scientifically safer and more reliable option. Deep drilling into deeper aquifers risks contaminating pristine subsurface water reserves, which would create irreversible long-term risk to both water and food systems. Given the current 18.8 months of food reserves, the colony can absorb the transition period if water use is tightly managed.
scienceBasis: Mars subsurface ice is confirmed, but deeper aquifer access carries planetary-protection contamination risk.
Mars atmospheric water vapor is sparse but recoverable with proven ISRU-style extraction heritage.
Reliability and contamination avoidance outweigh the faster but riskier drilling option.

commanderDecision.decision: Build the atmospheric water extraction system immediately and treat the next six months as a controlled austerity campaign: ration hard, monitor health daily, tighten the water loop, and hunt every leak like it is a hull breach.
commanderDecision.rationale: The colony is sitting on a live wire: 20% water deficit, morale at 10%, and high radiation burden. In that state, I will not gamble the settlement on a contamination-prone deep drill. High-upside leadership means protecting the engine of expansion, and water is the engine. The WAVAR path is slower, but it is proven, scalable, and does not risk poisoning a strategic reserve or triggering a planetary-protection failure. The dangerous move here is not choosing the slower option; the dangerous move is betting our future on a fragile experiment when the colony is already psychologically strained. We stabilize supply with reliable technology, preserve the aquifers for later, and use this six-month window to harden storage, improve recycling, and enforce discipline. Survival first, then acceleration.
selectedPolicies: Immediate construction of atmospheric water extraction capacity
Strict interim water rationing with priority allocation to life support, medical, and essential operations
Daily medical monitoring for hydration, renal strain, and fatigue
Leak detection and repair sweep across the entire water network
Recycling and storage optimization during deployment window
Defer deep aquifer drilling until sterilization and planetary-protection controls are materially stronger
rejectedPolicies: Immediate deep experimental drilling into deeper aquifers
Short-term water expansion that risks long-term contamination of subsurface reserves
Relaxed rationing during the six-month deployment gap
expectedTradeoffs: Morale may dip further in the short term because rationing is painful and discipline at 10% morale is hard to enforce
Reliable atmospheric extraction should improve long-term resilience and reduce existential pressure once online
We sacrifice speed for biosafety, avoiding a contamination event that could cripple future water strategy
Operational burden rises on medical and maintenance teams due to monitoring, leak detection, and enforcement
watchMetricsNextTurn: Net water deficit trend
Per-capita water consumption
Hydration compliance and medical dehydration indicators
Renal strain and fatigue markers
Water recycling efficiency
Storage losses and leak rate
WAVAR construction progress against the six-month schedule
Morale response to rationing discipline

policyEffectsApplied: Immediate construction of atmospheric water extraction capacity
Strict interim water rationing with priority allocation to life support, medical, and essential operations
Daily medical monitoring for hydration, renal strain, and fatigue
Leak detection and repair sweep across the entire water network
Recycling and storage optimization during deployment window
Defer deep aquifer drilling until sterilization and planetary-protection controls are materially stronger

departmentReports.summary: full hydroponic conversion.  Mars perchlorate contamination is a colony-wide ingestion hazard, and the safest health response is to remove soil from the food pathway entirely.

citations.text: Phoenix lander detected 0.5-1% calcium perchlorate in Mars soil globally
citations.context: Confirms the contamination problem is global, not local.

citations.text: Perchlorate is a thyroid toxin at chronic exposure above 0.7 ug/kg/day
citations.context: Supports treating crop ingestion as a serious chronic health risk.

citations.text: Perchlorate-reducing bacteria (Dechloromonas) can bioremediate contaminated soil
citations.context: Bioremediation is plausible, but still experimental for Mars operations.

citations.text: Bioremediation has not been tested in Mars atmospheric conditions
citations.context: Key uncertainty making bioremediation too risky as the primary food-safety strategy.

risks.description: Perchlorate exposure through food could cause chronic thyroid dysfunction across the colony if soil agriculture continues.
The colony’s morale is already extremely low; a 2-year uncertain remediation program could worsen food insecurity and psychological strain.
Hydroponic conversion requires 30% more power, which may stress the current energy budget and must be engineered carefully.

opportunities.description: Hydroponics offers immediate food safety control and predictable crop output without relying on uncertain soil remediation.
Sealed bays enable tighter control of humidity, pathogens, nutrients, and toxic exposure, improving medical oversight.
A hydroponic system can be scaled and standardized colony-wide, reducing future crop failure risk.

recommendedActions: Convert the colony to full hydroponic agriculture.
Purge all soil-grown food pathways from the diet until perchlorate exposure is eliminated.
Allocate the additional 120 kW power demand from nonessential loads and optimize lighting cycles.
Continue research on perchlorate bioremediation only as a long-term science program, not as the primary food safety solution.
Monitor thyroid function, urinary perchlorate exposure, and pediatric/endocrine vulnerability if any soil contact persists.

agriculture.reason: Most reliable immediate protection against thyroid toxin ingestion.

medicalSurveillance.endocrineMonitoring: Add routine thyroid labs and exposure tracking for all food-handling and crop-maintenance personnel.

forgedToolsUsed.name: perchlorate_agriculture_decision_model
forgedToolsUsed.description: Compares hydroponic conversion versus perchlorate bioremediation using validated colony inputs and estimated health-risk/reliability scoring.

openQuestions: How quickly can the habitat fabricators produce sealed hydroponic bay hardware at scale?
Can 120 kW of additional power be sustained without reducing critical life-support redundancy?
What crop mix will maximize nutrition per watt during the transition period?

recommendedEffects.description: Remove soil-based agriculture to eliminate perchlorate ingestion risk.
Invest in sealed hydroponic infrastructure to stabilize food production.
Retain bioremediation as a future experimental program rather than an operational dependency.

departmentReports.rationale: Mars surface regolith is globally contaminated with calcium perchlorate at levels confirmed by Phoenix, making direct open-soil agriculture unsafe without remediation. Given the colony’s immediate food-security needs, low morale, and the 2-year uncertainty of engineered bioremediation under Mars conditions, the scientifically stronger choice is to convert fully to sealed hydroponic production now. Hydroponics removes soil exposure entirely, avoids chronic perchlorate risk, and provides controllable yields with known operational parameters. The higher power cost is significant, but it is a known cost for a reliable food system; the alternative is an unproven biology program that could fail after two years and keep the colony food-insecure.
scienceBasis: Phoenix detected 0.5-1% calcium perchlorate in Mars soil globally.
Perchlorate is a thyroid toxin with well-established chronic exposure risk.
Dechloromonas bioremediation is promising on Earth but untested in Mars conditions.
Hydroponics eliminates soil contact and is the most reliable immediate mitigation.

commanderDecision.decision: Execute full hydroponic conversion immediately. Soil exits the food chain now. We will rewire agriculture into a sealed, power-hungry, controlled production system and treat bioremediation as a research flank, not a survival pillar.
commanderDecision.rationale: Perchlorate in the food pathway is a chronic poisoning problem, not an inconvenience. Medical confidence is high and the risk is critical: if we keep soil agriculture active, we invite colony-wide thyroid dysfunction and long-tail health damage. With morale at 7%, a two-year uncertain remediation gamble is exactly the wrong dependency. I favor upside, but only when the odds justify the thrust. Here, the highest-upside move is decisive containment: cut out the contaminated pathway, secure food safety, and absorb the engineering cost. Yes, hydroponics demands 30% more power and another 120 kW, but power strain is an engineering problem we can manage. Endocrine poisoning is a strategic degradation of the entire population. We choose control, predictability, and speed.
selectedPolicies: Immediate full conversion to sealed hydroponic agriculture
Immediate purge of all soil-grown food from colony diets
Reallocate 120 kW from nonessential loads to agricultural life support
Optimize lighting cycles and hydroponic energy efficiency
Begin endocrine and perchlorate exposure monitoring until all soil food pathways are confirmed closed
Continue perchlorate bioremediation only as a long-term R&D effort
rejectedPolicies: Maintaining soil agriculture while perchlorate contamination remains unresolved
Making engineered bioremediation the primary operational food safety plan
Accepting chronic perchlorate exposure in exchange for lower short-term power demand
expectedTradeoffs: Power availability tightens, forcing cuts or delays in lower-priority activities
Food safety improves sharply, reducing long-term medical burden and strategic health risk
Morale may recover modestly from decisive action and safer food, though transition stress remains real
Agricultural operations become more infrastructure-dependent and vulnerable to power disruptions
Bioremediation progress may slow because it is no longer mission-critical
watchMetricsNextTurn: Available power margin after 120 kW reallocation
Hydroponic conversion progress
Food output stability during transition
Urinary perchlorate exposure levels
Thyroid function indicators across the colony
Morale response to safer food supply
Failure rates in pumps, seals, and grow-bay control systems

policyEffectsApplied: Immediate full conversion to sealed hydroponic agriculture
Immediate purge of all soil-grown food from colony diets
Reallocate 120 kW from nonessential loads to agricultural life support
Optimize lighting cycles and hydroponic energy efficiency
Begin endocrine and perchlorate exposure monitoring until all soil food pathways are confirmed closed
Continue perchlorate bioremediation only as a long-term R&D effort

departmentReports.summary: Recommend Option B: accept 50 colonists.  Medically and operationally, this is the best balance between preserving colony stability and honoring Earth’s recruitment investment.

citations.text: NASA ECLSS regenerative life support on ISS supports 6-7 crew on ~11,000 kg system
citations.context: Shows how tightly life support scales and why rapid crew growth is operationally dangerous.

citations.text: Hohmann transfer window Earth-Mars occurs every 26 months with 6-9 month transit
citations.context: The arrival and expansion timing cannot be compressed enough to safely absorb a large influx.

citations.text: Mars habitat sizing for 100+ crew requires modular expandable architecture
citations.context: Supports gradual modular growth rather than a sudden population jump.

citations.text: Rapid population increase strains life support beyond designed capacity
citations.context: Directly supports avoiding the 200-person intake.

risks.description: Accepting all 200 colonists would overload life support beyond current rated capacity, risking oxygen rationing and acute stress-related health deterioration.
Crowding and inadequate environmental buffering would likely worsen morale, sleep, conflict, and adherence to safety protocols in an already fragile colony.
Declining all 200 could reduce Earth support and delay future expansion funding, indirectly harming long-term resilience and health security.

opportunities.description: Accepting 50 preserves safety margins while allowing controlled growth and visible commitment to expansion.
A smaller intake can be matched to housing, medical surveillance, and food/water capacity more reliably.
This option keeps political trust partially intact without forcing unsafe operational overreach.

recommendedActions: Accept 50 colonists now.
Fast-track modular life-support expansion and housing retrofits for the next phase.
Communicate a clear phased settlement plan to Earth to preserve political backing.
Prioritize medical screening, sleep scheduling, and conflict-prevention measures before arrival.
Reassess capacity only after the colony demonstrates stable performance above 120-person-equivalent support margins.

populationPolicy.reason: Maintains colony within survivable operational bounds.

medicalOperations.preparedness: Increase mental-health support, quarantine readiness, and environmental monitoring before arrival.

forgedToolsUsed.name: mars_population_life_support_risk_model
forgedToolsUsed.description: Compared population intake options against current life-support capacity and construction timing to estimate overload and stability risk.

openQuestions: Can life-support throughput be upgraded before the 14-month arrival date for the 50-person intake?
How much reserve oxygen and food buffering exists if construction slips by several months?
What contingency plan exists for quarantine, injury care, and crowding-related stress after arrival?

recommendedEffects.description: Accept 50 colonists to preserve safe population growth while maintaining operational control.
Launch accelerated modular life-support and housing expansion to prepare for the next growth phase.
Expand mental-health and conflict-prevention support to reduce crowding-related harm.

departmentReports.rationale: Accepting all 200 colonists would immediately push the colony far beyond its life-support rating of 120. Even if expansion is planned, the 14-month arrival timeline versus an 18-month construction timeline creates a guaranteed gap where oxygen, water, and food systems would be overstrained. Declining everyone would protect stability but would likely damage Earth support and future resupply. Accepting 50 is the only option that keeps the colony within current margins while allowing controlled, evidence-based growth and minor upgrades.
scienceBasis: ISS-style regenerative life support supports only small crews relative to habitat mass and complexity.
Mars habitats for 100+ crew require modular expandable architecture, not sudden population jumps.
The transfer window constraint makes full acceptance unsafe because construction cannot finish before arrival.
A limited intake preserves morale, political continuity, and crew safety.

departmentReports.rationale: The colony is already under severe psychological strain: morale is 11% in a 101-person settlement. Even though there are no current depressed cases, the system is brittle, and a 200-person influx would create major integration stress on top of life-support risk. With 101 people already near the 120-person ceiling, Option A is psychologically unsafe; a failure-induced rationing crisis would likely intensify anxiety, conflict, and despair. Option C best protects internal stability but risks external political fallout. Given the need to preserve both colony cohesion and Earth support, Option B is the least harmful compromise.

keyRisks: low morale amplifying rumor spread and conflict
rapid social integration failure from a large influx
stress contagion if life-support expansion slips
protectiveFactors: very small Mars-born subgroup means fewer entrenched generational tensions
psychologyAssessment.decisionLogic: The social system cannot safely absorb 200 newcomers in one transfer without meaningful risk of cohesion collapse. A smaller intake is more likely to preserve psychological stability while keeping Earth engaged.

advice: Communicate the decision as a capacity and safety constraint, not a rejection of personnel.
Prepare a staged integration plan and shared identity rituals for any accepted subgroup.
Increase mental health monitoring, especially for conflict markers, sleep disruption, and isolation behavior.
If politically possible, negotiate a deferred acceptance queue for the remaining colonists.

commanderDecision.decision: Accept 50 additional colonists now and frame it as Phase One of a staged expansion plan. We grow, but we do it with pressure seals intact: immediate life-support expansion, housing retrofits, and pre-arrival medical and psychological preparation.
commanderDecision.rationale: This is the right kind of aggressive move: controlled growth with a survivable buffer. Taking all 200 would slam straight into life-support limits and turn expansion into self-inflicted oxygen panic. Declining everyone would protect the short term but weaken Earth support, future funding, and strategic momentum. Accepting 50 threads the needle. It preserves political capital, increases labor capacity, and keeps the colony inside manageable operating margins while we build toward the next intake. My leadership style favors bold expansion, but not blind saturation. We should use this cohort as a force multiplier—new hands to accelerate infrastructure, maintenance depth, and social resilience—then reopen the gate only after support systems prove they can carry more than 120-person-equivalent loads without strain.
selectedPolicies: Accept 50 colonists as a phased expansion intake
Accelerate modular life-support expansion and housing retrofits immediately
Communicate a clear phased settlement roadmap to Earth to preserve confidence and funding
Conduct rigorous pre-arrival medical screening and arrival quarantine protocols
Implement structured sleep scheduling, mental-health support, and conflict-prevention measures before and after arrival
Set a hard threshold that larger intakes wait until stable performance above 120-person-equivalent capacity is demonstrated
rejectedPolicies: Expand population without simultaneous housing, life-support, and psychological support upgrades
expectedTradeoffs: Population growth increases crowding pressure and management complexity even at the reduced intake size
Morale should improve if expansion is orderly and visibly supported by infrastructure investment
Earth political backing is more likely to remain strong because we are growing rather than stalling
Resource consumption rises sooner, but so does labor capacity for construction, maintenance, and research
A phased plan may frustrate stakeholders pushing for faster expansion, but it prevents catastrophic overload
watchMetricsNextTurn: Life-support utilization versus rated capacity
Housing occupancy and environmental quality per person
Morale trend after intake announcement
Conflict incidents and behavioral health caseload
Sleep quality and schedule adherence
Medical screening outcomes for incoming colonists
Food and water consumption per capita
Progress on modular expansion milestones

policyEffectsApplied: Accept 50 colonists as a phased expansion intake
Accelerate modular life-support expansion and housing retrofits immediately
Communicate a clear phased settlement roadmap to Earth to preserve confidence and funding
Conduct rigorous pre-arrival medical screening and arrival quarantine protocols
Implement structured sleep scheduling, mental-health support, and conflict-prevention measures before and after arrival
Set a hard threshold that larger intakes wait until stable performance above 120-person-equivalent capacity is demonstrated

departmentReports.summary: Emergency order: evacuate all colonists into the reinforced core habitat immediately and seal all minimally shielded expansion modules.  With only 4 hours until CME impact, there is no time for partial mitigation or relocation risk.

citations.text: NASA radiation risk model establishes dose-response for astronaut cancer risk
citations.context: Supports aggressive minimization of additional radiation exposure.

citations.text: Mars lost its global magnetic field approximately 4 billion years ago
citations.context: Explains why the colony cannot rely on planetary shielding.

citations.text: September 2017 solar event measured by Curiosity RAD showed significant dose spike
citations.context: Confirms that Mars surface radiation can rise sharply during solar events.

risks.description: Any colonists remaining in minimally shielded modules may receive doses in the 100-500 mSv range over hours, causing acute blood-count changes and potentially radiation sickness.
Overcrowding the core habitat may increase stress, sleep disruption, and conflict, but these risks are far smaller than the radiation hazard.
Temporary shutdown of modules may interrupt operations, but maintaining human safety must override productivity during the event.

opportunities.description: This event is a clear trigger to formalize rapid solar-storm shelter protocols and occupancy drills.
If the colony survives with disciplined sheltering, confidence in emergency procedures will improve.
The crisis can motivate investment in more deeply shielded refuge capacity for future events.

recommendedActions: Move every colonist into the reinforced core habitat now.
Seal all expansion modules and suspend nonessential activity until the CME passes.
Account for every person immediately; no one should remain in a 5-10 g/cm² structure.
Prioritize medical observation for anyone who may have been exposed during transit or delay.
After the event, review shelter-capacity planning and expand hardened refuge space as soon as possible.

emergencyProtocol.reason: Maximizes shielding under severe time constraints.

medicalMonitoring.postEvent: Track CBC, fatigue, nausea, and skin/neurologic symptoms in all potentially exposed colonists.

forgedToolsUsed.name: cme_shelter_triage_model
forgedToolsUsed.description: Radiation shelter triage model intended to estimate exposure differences between reinforced core habitat and minimally shielded expansion modules during a Mars CME.

openQuestions: How many colonists are currently outside the core and how fast can they all be counted in?
Are there any exposed individuals who may already have received a pre-impact dose during transit between modules?
What is the core habitat’s maximum safe occupancy and can temporary life-support load be sustained for several days?

recommendedEffects.description: Order immediate full-colony sheltering in the reinforced core habitat.
Accelerate future construction of hardened storm refuge space.
Run a post-event stress debrief and reassurance protocol to reduce panic and improve compliance.

commanderDecision.decision: Execute immediate full-colony shelter-in-place in the reinforced core habitat. Seal all minimally shielded expansion modules, suspend nonessential operations, and run a hard accountability sweep until every colonist is physically confirmed under adequate shielding.
commanderDecision.rationale: There is no strategic ambiguity here. We have four hours and a high-confidence medical warning of potentially severe radiation exposure in lightly shielded modules. Productivity is irrelevant; survival is the mission. The right command decision is total consolidation, fast and disciplined. Overcrowding, friction, and temporary operational disruption are acceptable costs compared with avoidable radiation injury. We move now, verify every headcount, and accept no stragglers, no exceptions, no heroic nonsense. After the event, we convert this near miss into infrastructure doctrine by expanding hardened refuge capacity so the next storm does not force such a severe compression of the colony.
selectedPolicies: Immediate evacuation of all colonists into the reinforced core habitat
Seal minimally shielded expansion modules and suspend nonessential activity
Conduct immediate full accountability and transit verification for every colonist
Prioritize medical observation for anyone delayed in transit or potentially exposed
Launch post-event review and accelerated construction planning for additional hardened storm refuge space
Run post-event stress debrief and reassurance protocols to restore order and compliance
rejectedPolicies: Partial sheltering that leaves some personnel in lightly shielded modules
Maintaining normal operations during CME impact
Delaying evacuation to protect equipment, experiments, or productivity
expectedTradeoffs: Short-term morale may dip due to crowding, fear, and disrupted sleep
Radiation injuries and long-term health damage should be dramatically reduced
Operational losses and schedule delays are likely, but they are reversible
The event will expose shelter-capacity weaknesses, creating immediate pressure for capital reallocation toward hardened space
watchMetricsNextTurn: Confirmed radiation dose estimates by individual
Shelter occupancy density and habitability stress indicators
Headcount reconciliation and any transit-delay incidents
Post-event medical findings including blood-count changes
System restart readiness for sealed modules
Morale and panic-response trend after debrief
Timeline and resource requirements for hardened refuge expansion

policyEffectsApplied: Immediate evacuation of all colonists into the reinforced core habitat
Seal minimally shielded expansion modules and suspend nonessential activity
Conduct immediate full accountability and transit verification for every colonist
Prioritize medical observation for anyone delayed in transit or potentially exposed
Launch post-event review and accelerated construction planning for additional hardened storm refuge space
Run post-event stress debrief and reassurance protocols to restore order and compliance

policies: Immediate full conversion to sealed hydroponic agriculture

eventLog.description: Tera Kowalski born to Liam Kowalski and Luna Tanaka

Accept 50 additional colonists now and frame it as Phase One of a staged expansion plan. We grow, but we do it with pressure seals intact: immediate life-support expansion, housing retrofits, and pre-

policies: Accept 50 colonists as a phased expansion intake
Accelerate modular life-support expansion and housing retrofits immediately
Communicate a clear phased settlement roadmap to Earth to preserve confidence and funding
Conduct rigorous pre-arrival medical screening and arrival quarantine protocols
Implement structured sleep scheduling, mental-health support, and conflict-prevention measures before and after arrival
Set a hard threshold that larger intakes wait until stable performance above 120-person-equivalent capacity is demonstrated

eventLog.description: Execute immediate full-colony shelter-in-place in the reinforced core habitat. Seal all minimally shielded expansion modules, suspend nonessential operations, and run a hard accountability sweep until

policies: Immediate evacuation of all colonists into the reinforced core habitat
Seal minimally shielded expansion modules and suspend nonessential activity
Conduct immediate full accountability and transit verification for every colonist
Prioritize medical observation for anyone delayed in transit or potentially exposed
Launch post-event review and accelerated construction planning for additional hardened storm refuge space
Run post-event stress debrief and reassurance protocols to restore order and compliance
